coredata.dc:description: In the current century, the rapid growth of the global population has significantly heightened the demand for energy and escalated waste production. This study aims to develop a technology that efficiently addresses both energy demand and waste management by creating nanofluids from production waste or byproducts, assessing their effectiveness through experimental measurements and simulations. A comprehensive investigation into the thermophysical properties and heat transfer performance of carbon-black-based nanofluids is presented. Carbon-black, a byproduct of petroleum combustion, is used to formulate nanofluids due to its superior thermal conductivity. The experimental analysis involves varying the weight concentrations (0.005 %, 0.01 %, 0.02 %, and 0.03 %), temperature (35°C to 55°C in 5°C increments), and particle sizes (30 nm, 80 nm, and 200 nm) to evaluate their effects on thermal conductivity, viscosity, specific heat, and density. A correlation model is developed to predict the temperature-dependent thermophysical properties, leveraging the Response Surface Methodology (RSM) and a 3k factorial design with center points. This model facilitates the optimization of control factors, crucial for enhancing the nanofluid's heat transfer capabilities. Additionally, Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) simulations incorporate these correlations to provide dynamic, temperature-dependent analyses of the nanofluids, enhancing the realism and accuracy of the simulation outcomes. The research further explores the application of carbon-black nanofluids in a microchannel heat exchanger system, assessing the heat transfer performance through experimental and simulated approaches. Initial findings indicate that the carbon-black nanofluid enhances thermal conductivity by up to 0.9 % at minimal weight concentrations and temperatures, alongside a viscosity increase of 22.8 % at a 45°C and 0.01 % weight concentration. Optimizing the operational conditions to 45°C and 0.01 % weight concentration significantly improves the heat transfer rate while reducing pumping power requirements, yielding performance indexes showing improvements of 9.05 %, 7.80 %, and 8.3 % for the respective particle sizes compared to distilled water. The study confirms that carbon-black-based nanofluids not only enhance heat transfer efficiency but also promote sustainability by valorizing a petroleum byproduct, thus contributing to energy reuse and environmental conservation in industrial applications.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the current century, the rapid growth of the global population has significantly heightened the demand for energy and escalated waste production. This study aims to develop a technology that efficiently addresses both energy demand and waste management by creating nanofluids from production waste or byproducts, assessing their effectiveness through experimental measurements and simulations. A comprehensive investigation into the thermophysical properties and heat transfer performance of carbon-black-based nanofluids is presented. Carbon-black, a byproduct of petroleum combustion, is used to formulate nanofluids due to its superior thermal conductivity. The experimental analysis involves varying the weight concentrations (0.005 %, 0.01 %, 0.02 %, and 0.03 %), temperature (35°C to 55°C in 5°C increments), and particle sizes (30 nm, 80 nm, and 200 nm) to evaluate their effects on thermal conductivity, viscosity, specific heat, and density. A correlation model is developed to predict the temperature-dependent thermophysical properties, leveraging the Response Surface Methodology (RSM) and a 3k factorial design with center points. This model facilitates the optimization of control factors, crucial for enhancing the nanofluid's heat transfer capabilities. Additionally, Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) simulations incorporate these correlations to provide dynamic, temperature-dependent analyses of the nanofluids, enhancing the realism and accuracy of the simulation outcomes. The research further explores the application of carbon-black nanofluids in a microchannel heat exchanger system, assessing the heat transfer performance through experimental and simulated approaches. Initial findings indicate that the carbon-black nanofluid enhances thermal conductivity by up to 0.9 % at minimal weight concentrations and temperatures, alongside a viscosity increase of 22.8 % at a 45°C and 0.01 % weight concentration. Optimizing the operational conditions to 45°C and 0.01 % weight concentration significantly improves the heat transfer rate while reducing pumping power requirements, yielding performance indexes showing improvements of 9.05 %, 7.80 %, and 8.3 % for the respective particle sizes compared to distilled water. The study confirms that carbon-black-based nanofluids not only enhance heat transfer efficiency but also promote sustainability by valorizing a petroleum byproduct, thus contributing to energy reuse and environmental conservation in industrial applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent decades, there has been significant economic growth of global nations, elevating of living standards and a subsequent increase in the energy demand. The increase in energy consumption has heightened the need for the investigation and integration of alternative energy sources. This trend is driven by the escalating energy needs of modern society and the expanding global population [1]. According to the United Nations' Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), the advancement of net zero technologies that efficiently capture and recycle energy resources represents a crucial and advantageous development. The enhancement of by-products in the chemical industry and their subsequent repurposing as innovative materials, such as nanofluids, holds promise for numerous benefits. Recently, nanofluids have gathered considerable interest in the field of energy acquisition, owing to their high potential for augmenting heat transfer and thermal properties [2]. Nanofluids demonstrate improved thermal performance through the dispersion of solid nanoparticles with diameters ranging from 1 to 100 nm [3] into base fluids. This enhancement in properties is attributed to the synergistic interaction between the nanoparticles and the base fluid. Nanoparticles have been systematically categorized based on their origin, dimensions, chemical composition, and morphology to emphasize their unique characteristics [4]. Nanoparticles are added to base-fluid such as water in order to enhance heat transfer efficiency, owing to their well high thermal conductivity as depicted in Table S1 in supplementary material [5]. Nanosized particles, as demonstrated in Table S1 in supplementary material, exhibit significantly higher thermal conductivity compared to the base fluid, making it advantageous to enhance the heat transfer efficiency of the base fluid by incorporating nanofluids. In the assessment of nanofluids as potential working fluids, it is imperative to consider not only their thermal conductivity properties but also other relevant characteristics including viscosity, density, and specific heat. The analysis of heat transfer in working fluids involves the consideration of two primary phenomena: heat conduction in the static medium like material and heat convection in the presence of medium motion like working fluid. Thermophysical properties play a crucial role in facilitating the understanding of these hydrothermal phenomena both heat transfer and hydrodynamics. These properties serve as parameters that aid in the assessment and evaluation of hydrothermal processes within working fluids. There are many studies investigated the relevant properties based on heat transfer prospect in several nanofluid. Table 1 presents data collected from a report detailing research conducted on water-based nanofluids. Water, utilized as the base fluid in the nanofluid, offers several advantages due to its widespread availability and superior thermal conductivity compared to other commonly used base fluids, as presented in Table S1 in supplementary material. The thermal conductivity of nanofluids is a property that has gained significant attention in research due to the influence of solid particles. Previous studies have demonstrated that the dispersion of nanoparticles in a water medium can greatly enhance thermal conductivity by up to 25 % referred to previous works from researchers in Table 1 on the thermal conductivity section. Furthermore, it is crucial to evaluate the influence of other factors which has an influence on heat transfer performance, particularly viscosity. The dispersion of nanoparticles within a medium would result in an increase in viscosity due to the interactions between the nanoparticles and the medium, which can be attributed to both solid-solid and solid-fluid interactions. According to studies referred to previous works from researchers in Table 1 on the viscosity section, it has been noted that the viscosity of the medium can increase by as much as 180 % because of the presence of nanoparticles within it. Specific heat can be viewed as a complementary parameter to thermal conductivity in the analysis of temperature gradients within a material. It is defined as the quantity of heat energy needed to increase the temperature of one unit mass of a substance by one degree Celsius [6]. Based on the findings of previous research (references from previous works of researchers on the specific heat section as listed in Table 1), it has been observed that the specific heat of the medium can potentially decrease by up to 45 %. Density can be scientifically defined as the measure of mass per unit volume of a substance [7]. It has been observed that denser fluids tend to flow at a slower rate in comparison to less dense fluids. The denser fluids have a higher concentration of molecules, leading to an increased resistance to flow as the fluid moves. On Table 1 in the density section have indicated that the density of a medium can potentially increase by up to 10 % due to the presence of nanoparticles within it.

As thermal properties of a nanofluid are primarily influenced by the dispersed particles, making it essential to consider carbon-based nanoparticles. In addition to their elevated thermal conductivity [8], these nanoparticles have also been reported to possess a high aspect ratio [9], heightened stability [10], and reduced susceptibility to erosion and corrosion on their surface [11]. Carbon-based nanofluids, such as carbon nanotubes (CNTs), graphene, and graphite, have garnered significant attention in various fields for their diverse applications and versatility. Despite carbon-black (CB) exhibiting the lowest thermal conductivity among these materials, it presents an intriguing alternative to the commonly used metal oxide-based nanofluids. Carbon-black is primarily generated through the incomplete combustion of dense petroleum derivatives such as FCC tar, coal tar, ethylene cracking tar, and certain vegetable oils [12]. There is a widely used amorphous carbon nanomaterial with different grades available, each tailored to specific applications based on factors such as aggregate size, distribution, morphology, and surface activity [13]. Recently, carbon-black has been examined as a flame retardant for polypropylene and linear low-density polyethylene. The studies have shown that the addition of carbon-black leads to notable improvements in the thermal stability of these polymers [14,15]. The utilization of carbon-black in the development of nanofluids has emerged as a promising method for enhancing heat transfer efficiency and utilizing waste heat, thereby addressing the growing energy demand challenge. Consequently, numerous recent studies have concentrated on this area of research. Firoozzadeh et al. [16] explored the use of nanofluid circulation, specifically utilizing carbon-black nanoparticles in water, as a coolant for photovoltaic modules. Experimental investigation revealed that a concentration of 0.21 % by weight of carbon-black nanoparticles in water resulted in 7 % more output power compared to pure water coolant and 54 % more output power compared to conventional photovoltaic modules. Han et al. [17] investigated the preparation and characterization of carbon-black nanofluids by dispersing pretreated carbon-black powder into distilled water. The study explored the size, morphology, photothermal properties, optical properties, rheological behaviors, and thermal conductivities of the nanofluids. Results indicated that nanofluids with high-volume fractions demonstrate superior photothermal properties and absorption abilities across a wide wavelength range. Wei et al [18]. utilized carbon-black (CB) nanofluids as the working fluids in a rectangular direct absorption solar collectors (DASC), investigating thermal performance under varying flow rates and tilt angles with a concentrated simulated solar power source. Experimental results indicated that increasing flow rate improved DASC efficiency, with optimal efficiency observed at a tilt angle of 180 ° and a nanoparticle concentration of 0.05 % by weight. As previously discussed, carbon-black nanofluid has been considered as a potential candidate for use in hydrothermal applications, even though it is primarily utilized in the field of photovoltaics.

To evaluate the performance the numerical and computational calculation are the useful tool for predictive investigating and indicating the hydrothermal system. The well-known Computational Fluid Dynamic (CFD) simulation is an exclusive technique to study in fluid flow that considered both hydrodynamic and heat transfer. Since the nanofluid is the new innovated coolant, so it should be more investigated insight and CFD is a suitable method to be usage. There are many studies had investigated nanofluid heat transfer performance by using CFD simulation. Khalifa et al. [19] studied on a photovoltaic thermal using bi-fluid by used 3D CFD simulation analysis. They utilized Al2O3/Water nanofluid on photovoltaic-thermal (PVT) collector system for enhancing cooling performance. The study results achieved the total thermal efficiency of PVT modules with the bifluid is equal to 46.63 %, 48.96 %, 52.39 %, 54.13 %, 59.43 %, and 63.28 %, for Al2O3/water nanofluids concentrations of 0.0, 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8, and 1 %, respectively. Ahmed et al. [20] investigated the Al2O3/water nanofluid heat transfer application in dimpled minichannels by using CFD analysis. The study considered 0 %, 1 %, and 4 % volume concentration of Al2O3/water nanofluid flowing in 50 mm diameter cylinder 110 mm in length by varying Re 250 to 1000 with 250 increments. The results suggested that the dimpled channel combined 1 % volume fraction of alumina nano-fluid could increase up to 29 %, and 4 % volume fraction of alumina nano-fluid could upsurge up to 32 % on thermal efficiency. Salameh et al. [21] also studied in the inside concentric counter flow tube heat exchanger together with using 0.05 and 0.2 volume fraction of CuO, Al2O3, and TiO2 nanofluids by analyzed on computational fluid dynamics simulation. The 69 cm in length and 1.27 cm and 2.45 cm for inner and outer diameters concentric tube heat exchanger was used to be a heat exchanged system by using 60 °C nanofluids as hot working fluids at inside and water as coolant at outside. Their findings were presented that the best performance index was achieved at Re = 2000 on the considered range Re 2000 to 12,000. The results also suggested that both 0.05 and 0.2 volume fraction of nanofluids provided equally the best performance index by CuO/water with 4.25, Al2O3/water with 3, and TiO2/water with 2.5 compared to water.

To devise a technology that proficiently meets both energy requirements and waste management, this study aims to thoroughly investigate value-adding side product chemicals in carbon-black water-based nanofluids within the framework of net-zero technology development. Initially, the research focuses on characterizing the thermophysical properties of these nanofluids. Measurements of thermal conductivity, viscosity, specific heat, and density are conducted at varying concentrations (0.005 %, 0.01 %, 0.02 %, and 0.03 % by weight) of nanometer-sized carbon-black particles compared to distilled water. These properties provide the foundation for developing a preliminary predictive model using the Response Surface Methodology (RSM), aiming to optimize the balance between heat transfer enhancements and hydrodynamic resistance. Subsequently, the study employs Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) simulations, specifically through the ANSYS Fluent 2023 R1 software package, to assess the hydrothermal performance of these nanofluids in a microchannel heat exchanger system. This simulation incorporates a user-defined function to apply the temperature-dependent nanofluid thermophysical properties derived from the experimental results, enhancing the accuracy of the model. The research methodology embodies a comprehensive cycle of experimental measurement, statistical analysis, and simulation. Starting with the experimental characterization of a nanofluid comprising unknown nanoparticles and a base fluid, the study progresses through statistical evaluation to refine the potential applications of the nanofluid. It culminates with a detailed CFD analysis to provide insights into the nanofluid behavior at the microscale. This approach not only suggests optimal performance configurations for heat transfer applications but also contributes to a sustainable cycle of energy consumption and waste management by utilizing byproducts in the formulation of the nanofluid.

Carbon-black (CB) nanoparticles utilized in this study were sourced from by-products of real petroleum distillation industry. The carbon-black was processed by petroleum feedstock and was processed by manufacturing process such as partial combustion and thermal cracking [12]. The nanoparticles were obtained in three distinct types: two fine particle filter-sized varieties designated as Line 1, an unfiltered-sized variant denoted as Line 2, and one coarse variant that did not undergo refinement procedures as displayed in Fig. 1 . The initial phase involves an analysis of the dimensions of carbon-black to ascertain the presence of particles at the nanometer scale. This assessment is carried out through the utilization of Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) and Energy Dispersive X-ray Spectrometer - SEM-EDS model FESEM JSM-7610F. Fig. 2 presents a visual representation of the scanned images obtained through SEM including a) Line 1 (x 1000), b) Line 1 (x 50,000), c) Line 2 (x 50,000) and d) Coarse (x 50,000). Fig. 2a displays an image of carbon-black particles as captured using scanning electron microscopy at a magnification of 1,000x. The image reveals a complex arrangement of intertwined particles. By increasing the magnification to 50,000x, the primary particle size of a carbon-black particle was depicted in Fig. 1b and c. The primary particle size of carbon-black in both Line 1 and Line 2 was found to be less than 100 nanometers, as indicated by a white tag in the bottom right corner of each image. Fig. 2d depicts particles classified as Coarse, with sizes exceeding 100 nm, as indicated by the presence of a white tag in the image. Despite surpassing the size indicated by the white tag, the Coarse particles remain within the nanometer range. The SEM images in Fig. 2 can confirm the particle size of carbon-black powders (Line 1, Line 2, and Coarse) are in nanometer. By analyzing the SEM images in scientific software ImageJ, it can be indicated that the mean equivalent diameters of the nanopowders were 30 nm ± 10 nm in Line 1, 80 nm ± 10 nm in Line 2, and 200 nm ± 10 in Coarse. According to the analysis depicted in Fig. 2, a significant proportion of the particles observed demonstrate a spherical morphology, while a smaller number displays a tubular shape within Line 2 and Coarse. This observation suggests the presence of contaminants, which could potentially have an adverse impact on the thermophysical properties of the nanofluid under investigation.

In order to effectively disperse carbon-black nanoparticles in water, careful consideration must be given to the preparation of the solution. Acetone, an organic compound possessing qualities similar to water, such as a neutral pH of 7, yet exhibiting lower surface tension, is being considered as a potential surfactant in the present study. The investigation conducted in this study focuses on the characterization of acetone in relation to its interaction with carbon-black nanoparticles through Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR). The carbon-black samples were analyzed by FTIR (Perkin Elmer model Spectrum One). Fig. 3 displays the FTIR spectra of carbon-black (from Line 1) in terms of %transmittance and absorbance that used to investigate the presence of chemical bonding. The absorption bands exhibited weak intensity due to the intrinsic black-body absorbing properties of carbon-black [42]. The band observed at 2000 - 2200 cm−1 in both carbon-black and carbon-black with acetone (liquid-phase) would represent the characteristic of C = C conjugated and C C [43]. The absorption spectra of carbon-black in the presence of acetone exhibited a noticeable shift towards lower wavelengths compared to the spectra of carbon-black alone. This observation is supported by studies from Zappielo et al. [44] that grafted carbon black with 3-mercaptopropyltrimethoxysilane (3-MPTMS) and Jaberi et al. [45] that modified carbon black with citric acid. Both studies reported spectra shifts toward lower wavelengths for modified carbon black. These findings corroborate the trend observed in the current study where the presence of acetone induces a similar spectral shift. This shift can be attributed to the interaction between acetone and the carbon-black material. A prominent peak corresponding to the C=O functional group of acetones typically falls within the range of 1600 – 1800 cm−1 [43,46,47]. To visualize the C=O band more clearly, Fig. 3b illustrates the absorbance results obtained from the FTIR spectra. The presence of a peak in the spectrum indicates the presence of acetone within the carbon-black sample. The characterization of acetone in the carbon-black material will be further elucidated in Section 3, focusing on the preparation of nanofluids.

Carbon-black nanoparticles in water-based nanofluids are synthesized through the direct dispersion of carbon-black nanoparticles in water. The efficacy of this method was evaluated by testing the carbon-black nanoparticles directly dispersed in distilled water produced through water purification. The results indicated that the carbon-black nanoparticles exhibited a floating behavior, as illustrated in Fig. 4 a. This observation may be attributed to the presence of carbon bonds, as evidenced in Fig. 3. Fig. 5 presents a diagram depicting the formation of C-C bonds between carbon-black nanoparticles into a carbon sheet [16,46]. The tight molecular bonding environment, such as water, facilitates the transformation of carbon-black nanoparticles into a carbon sheet, resulting in a structure that is difficult to disperse. There are two potential methods for enhancing the dispersion of carbon-black nanoparticles within the water environment including reducing surface tension and breaking the C-C bond by chemical modification. Numerous studies have employed various surfactants, including sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), and sodium dodecyl benzoic sulfate (SDBs), in their research [47,48,49,50]. The cohesive forces between water molecules exhibit significant strength, resulting in the inherent high surface tension of water. When surfactants are introduced into water, the intermolecular forces between surfactant molecules and water molecules are comparatively weaker than the forces between two water molecules. This weakening of intermolecular forces results in a decrease in surface tension, which in turn facilitates the dispersion of nanoparticles within the water medium [51]. Another method is breaking the C-C bond by using a chemical reaction. As shown in Fig. 5 when adding a chemical solution that is composed of hydroxyl groups (-OH), the hydroxide ion (OH-) will chemically bind to carbon atoms, resulting in the disruption of carbon-carbon bonds. This process possibly leads to the fragmentation of carbon atoms, which subsequently recombine with other fragmented carbons to form carbon nanoparticles [52]. Acetone presents as a promising candidate due to its ketone group (C=O) composition and compatibility with a neutral pH of 7. The oxygen atom within the ketone group can engage in carbon bonding similar to a hydroxyl group, ultimately resulting in the breakdown of carbon agglomeration to form carbon-black nanoparticles, as illustrated in Fig. 5. In addition, acetone not only provides a bond-breaking mechanism but also reduces the water surface tension. Wu et al. [53] and investigated the impact of varying acetone molar ratios in water solvents on surface tension measurements. The findings revealed that the addition of less than 1 molar of acetone resulted in a significant reduction in surface tension from 72 mN/m to approximately 40 mN/m. Furthermore, it was observed that surface tension decreased to 24.5 mN/m when the acetone concentration reached 20 molar ratios, approaching the surface tension of pure acetone. Nonomura et al. [54] and Shen et al. [50] proposed that acetone plays a role in promoting the disintegration of carbon-based particles, such as graphene, into smaller fragments through the process of sonication. This results in the dispersion of the particles, which is maintained by electrostatic repulsion between the charged surfaces. The particles remain dispersed due to electrostatic repulsion between the charged surfaces. As such, acetone emerges as a potentially advantageous reagent for aiding in the dispersion of carbon-black nanoparticles. However, it is essential to bear in mind the potential toxicity of acetone when contemplating its application on a larger scale. The carbon-black nanofluid (sample B) was prepared by adding 1 mL of acetone into water, as depicted in Fig. 4b. To ensure proper dispersion of the carbon-black nanofluid mixture, sonication was carried out for a duration of 3 hours using a GT SONIC device. The sonication process involved a cycle of running for 5 minutes followed by a 5-minute pause to prevent overheating. The dispersed nanofluid (sample C) is shown in Fig. 4c.

Four concentrations of carbon-black nanofluid, 0.005 %, 0.01 %, 0.02 %, and 0.03 % by weight, were prepared by carefully measuring three distinct types of carbon-black nanoparticles (referred to as Line 1, Line 2, and Coarse) using a precision digital scale with a readability of 0.0001 g and an error margin of ± 0.0002 g (Ohaus model PX-224). Subsequently, the nanoparticles were transferred into a 30-ml vial (ALWSCI) containing 1 mL of acetone and the appropriate amount of water to achieve the desired concentration level. To investigate a method for achieving uniform dispersion of carbon-black nanoparticles, the present study involved immersing 1 gram of the nanoparticles in a small quantity of acetone solvent (2 - 3 ml). The resulting test sample was allowed to sit in a room temperature environment until the acetone had completely evaporated, which took approximately one week.

This research focuses on developing a new method to accurately quantify the heat-related properties of innovative fluids. This is crucial because many recently developed fluids, like those containing tiny carbon particles, lack sufficient data in existing records. The proposed self-measurement method presents a timely and advantageous solution for generating essential data.

The heat transfer and flow behavior of fluids significantly impact how well they work in various systems. The collected results have the potential to provide valuable insights into the changes in the performance of nanofluids. This data can be utilized to gain a better understanding of the underlying mechanisms driving the observed trends in nanofluid behavior. This study aims to understand how three key factors – the amount of nanoparticles added (0.005 % to 0.03 % by weight), the temperature (between 35°C and 55°C), and the type of carbon nanoparticles used (Line 1, Line 2, and Coarse.

The thermal conductivity of carbon-black/water nanofluids was experimentally determined using a transient hot wire method, which is commonly used for measuring the thermal conductivity of electrically non-conducting materials such as soil, bulk solids, noble gases, or organic liquids. The transient hot wire method operates on the principle of resistance temperature detection (RTD), which tracks changes in the electrical resistance of conductive metals, such as platinum, copper, and nickel, as a function of temperature [55,56]. In this technique, the metal wire functions simultaneously as both a temperature sensor and a heat source. By analyzing variations in the wire's resistance in response to temperature changes, this method accurately measures the temperature of the surrounding medium, utilizing the predictable relationship between temperature and electrical resistance characteristic of RTD systems. The determination of thermal conductivity involves solving the transient heat conduction equation within a medium that houses a linear thermal source. The assessment of nanofluid thermal conductivity (k) is commonly carried out through the utilization of Eq. (1) as provided

The thermal conductivity of the nanofluid was assessed using a custom-built device based on the transient hot-wire method. Departing from conventional approaches where a single metal wire serves both as a temperature sensor and heat source, this study employed two distinct nickel wire K-type thermocouples. Nickel wire was chosen for its thermal and electrical properties, which are similar to those of platinum and copper, and for its cost-effectiveness and convenience [57,58,59]. One thermocouple measured the temperature of the sample, while the other, coupled with an 80 W SUS304 cartridge heater, was used to monitor and regulate the temperature of the heat source. A schematic diagram and image illustrating the setup of the measuring system can be found in Fig. 6 . The device was designed to be attached to a 30 ml vial to contain the sample within the measuring system, as depicted in Fig. 6a and b. The experimental setup ensured a constant temperature environment for the sample during measurements, utilizing a thermostatic bath to simulate an infinite medium, as illustrated in Fig. 6c and d. The initial step in the experimental procedure involved immersing the sample vial in a thermostatic bath for a duration of 1 minute to measure the temperature of the nanofluid sample. Subsequently, once the temperature of the sample had reached a stable state, the heater was activated, and the heating power was regulated to the desired target value using a voltage controller. The heating power was set to 40 % of the total 230 V voltage, yielding a power output of 13 Watts. By maintaining a constant heater power, we aim to minimize deviations caused by variable electrical resistance during measurements, allowing for stable application of the parameters in Eq. (1). The K-type thermocouples obtained from Phidget Inc were connected to a laptop via a data logger. The data was recorded in real-time using the Phidget control panel software. The dataset contained timestamps in milliseconds and temperature measurements with precision to three decimal places. Measurements were carried out three times for each test fluid. As a result, the average value of subsequent measurements was adopted.

The measurement was planned to measure the thermal conductivity of the nanofluid within a temperature range of 35°C to 55°C, with temperature increments of 5°C. To ensure the reliability of the measurements, the apparatus will be calibrated by assessing the thermal conductivity of water and comparing the results with reference values which will be reported with nanofluid thermal conductivity.

Specific heat can be described as the amount of heat (measured in joules) that is absorbed by a unit mass (measured in kilograms) of a material, resulting in a temperature increase of 1 Kelvin (K) or 1 ° Celsius (°C) [60]. The specific heat of water and carbon-black nanofluid was also determined using the modified transient hot-wire method outlined in Section 3.1.1. A self-designed device was utilized to measure specific heat, with modifications made to the method originally used for measuring thermal conductivity in order to adapt to the new requirements. The experimental setup involved the use of a vial holder, as depicted in Fig. 6c, to suspend a 30 ml sample vial. In accordance with the definition of specific heat, the testing environment involved heating the sample using a cartridge heater and monitoring its temperature using a K-type thermocouple, as illustrated in Fig. 6b.

In this study, specific heat (CP ), sample weight (m), and sample temperature change (ΔT) were denoted by respective symbols. The input heat (Q) was determined by the voltage applied to the heater and the electric current passing through it. The heat capacity of the heat-insulating vessel (W) was assessed through a process of heating distilled water in the vessel, measuring the resulting temperature rise, and calculating the vessel's heat capacity based on the known specific heat of distilled water. The gradient of the ratio of input heat to temperature change (Q/ΔT) was determined by conducting a linear regression analysis on the measured data.

To minimize heat loss, the 30 mL vial was insulated with elastomeric tape. The measurements were taken over a period of 3 minutes until reached the constant value and repeated 3 times for each loop of the experiment, with subsequent readings averaged to obtain the final value. To ensure the reliability of the measurements, the apparatus will be calibrated by assessing the specific heat of water and comparing the results with reference values which will be reported with nanofluid specific heat. As described in Section 3.1.1. thermal conductivity measurement, the heating power of the heater was adjusted to 40 % of the total voltage of 230V, yielding a power output of 13 Watts. K-type thermocouples acquired from Phidget Inc were utilized and connected to a laptop through a data logger. Real-time data recording was conducted using the Phidget control panel software, with the dataset containing timestamps in milliseconds and temperature readings with a precision of three decimal places.

Viscosity can be defined as the ratio of shearing stress to the velocity gradient within a given unit area [61]. The carbon-black nanofluid viscosity measurements were performed using a programmable rotational cylindrical viscometer Brookfield model DV-2T with low-range viscosity spindle code ULA. The principal measurement of the force/torque proportional to the viscous drag of the fluid acting on the spindle is determined by the deflection of a spring measured using a rotary transducer. The torque required to rotate the cylindrical spindle within the sample at a specific angular velocity serves as the foundation for this experimental setup. The theoretical framework for this type of viscometer, as illustrated in Fig. 7 a, can be described, with the formulation for Newtonian fluids derived in Eq. (3).

The viscometer (Fig. 7b) was utilized in this study to quantify the viscosity of a water and carbon-black nanofluid at varying particle weight concentrations and temperatures. The sample holder employed was the ULA model, equipped with a temperature control jacket that was linked to a circulating water bath to regulate the water temperature within the range of 35 to 55°C. All viscosity measurements were performed by subjecting the nanofluid to different rotational speeds of the ULA spindle, ranging from 10 to 200 rpm with 10 rpm increments, until a stable viscosity reading was achieved within the torque acceptable in range of 10 % to 100 %. Each measurement was repeated three times to ensure consistency, and the average value of the recorded readings will be reported.

Density is operationally defined as the measure of an object's mass per unit volume [62]. By the definition, the density of the carbon-black nanofluid was assessed through the utilization of the pycnometric method. A pycnometer with a volume of 10.291 cm3 and a measuring uncertainty of 0.001 cm3 was utilized for this purpose. The weighing process was carried out using a high precision analytical balance model Ohaus model PX-224 with a precision digital scale with a readability of 0.0001 g and an error margin of ± 0.0002 g. The density of the nanofluid was calculated utilizing the appropriate mathematical equation.

Experimental investigations on the thermophysical properties of water and carbon-black nanofluids necessitate a high degree of measurement accuracy to ensure the correct interpretation of results. It is imperative to conduct an uncertainty analysis to provide a quantitative assessment of the validity of the experimental findings. The determination of uncertainties in key parameters such as mass nanoparticle concentration and density is crucial, as these are directly measured variables. A fundamental procedure is employed to ascertain the uncertainty, whereby the mean relative uncertainty of a complex quantity Y, represented as Y = Y(X1, X2,…, Xi), is calculated following a general mathematical relation.

In the analysis of viscosity and thermal conductivity, precise measurements were conducted using specialized equipment, contributing to the accuracy of the results. The uncertainties associated with the viscosity (µ) and thermal conductivity (k) values are a direct reflection of the precision of the measuring instruments employed. Additionally, temperature measurements were carried out using high-quality temperature sensors with specified accuracies. Table 2 provides the measurement uncertainties of the parameters for reference and further analysis.

The Response Surface Methodology (RSM) is a valuable tool for constructing predictive models by examining the relationship between control factors, their interactions, and responses within statistical data [63]. This involves the fitting of mathematical functions, such as linear and polynomial equations, to the data [64]. In this study, an investigation is conducted on the influence of % weight concentration, temperature, and particle size (type of particle) on heat transfer and fluid flow. The responses are categorized into thermal conductivity, specific heat, viscosity, and density. All design parameters and their respective ranges can be found in Table 3 , which illustrates the range of each parameter. The % weight concentration ranges from 0 to 0.03 % by 0.01 % increment. The temperature varies between 35 °C (the normal room temperature in Thailand which around 30 °C in winter to 40 °C in summer) and 55 °C with 5 °C increase. The particle size considers in three various types of carbon-black nanoparticle that are denoted in Line 1, Line 2 and Coarse. The variation of these parameters aids in determining proper nanofluid conditions to optimal performance and cost worthy.

Three–level experimental design is selected which is accounted for two numerical factors and one categorical factor [64]. The numerical factors are two control factors are % weight concentration and temperature, and categorical factor is a particle type or size.

Three–level experimental design running case:
(6)
N =
3
k

The conventional three-level design is structured in the form of a 3k factorial design together with center points, whereby k factors are examined at three distinct levels denoted as low, intermediate, and high. These levels are typically represented numerically as 0, 1, and 2. Although an alternative representation using -1, and +1 could have been considered, this may lead to confusion as is commonly reserved for center points in 2-level designs. Thus, the decision to adopt the 1, 2 scheme was made to avoid ambiguity. The rationale behind the introduction of this design is to account for potential curvature in the response function and to accommodate nominal factors at three levels. Additionally, including a third level for continuous factors allows for the examination of quadratic relationships between the response variable and each factor [65].

To evaluate the performance of carbon-black nanofluid, a computational fluid dynamics (CFD) model was employed to simulate its application. The hydrothermal characteristics of the counterflow plate microchannel heat exchanger were considered. The measured thermophysical properties of carbon-black will be adopted on cold side, a set of assumptions has been employed.

Constant solid properties (Copper)

The flow in the computational domain is three-dimensional, laminar, incompressible, and steady. The thermal boundary condition for the external wall was adiabatic, and there was a conjugate heat transfer system between the fluid at 30°C on the cold side and hot water at 80°C on the hot side and the solid (copper) referred to Zhou et al. The plate microchannel heat exchanger geometry and dimensions were presented in Fig. 8 and Table S2 in Supplementary material.

Pumping power is a fundamental parameter within the field of fluid dynamics that serves to quantify the energy efficiency of pump systems, thereby representing the requisite power for the operation of such systems. It is calculated as the product of the pressure drop (ΔP) and the volumetric flow rate, denoted as
Q ˙
. This metric is crucial in the analysis and optimization of pump performance and is essential for ensuring the effective and efficient operation of fluid handling systems.

The considered thermal properties included thermal conductivity and specific heat of water and carbon-black water-based nanofluid as the results presented in Figs. 9 and 10
, respectively. In Figs. 9 and 10, the tag a, b, and c referred to Line 1, Line 2, and Coarse, respectively. The measured results of the water in this study were represented by black square symbols (▪), while the reference values were denoted by cross symbols (×). The result of the carbon-black nanofluids were denoted as followed 0.005 % weight in triangle symbol (▴), 0.01 % weight in circle symbol (ο), 0.02 % weight in diamond symbol (◊), 0.03 % weight in white square symbol (□). Thermal conductivity is a fundamental property that is often associated with heat transfer through the process of heat conduction [72]. In general, solids like nanoparticle carbon-black in this case possess higher thermal conductivity than fluids such as water in this study, as evidenced by the data presented in Table S1 in supplementary material. Nanofluids are increasingly being recognized as a viable option for utilizing the exceptional thermal conductivity properties of mixed solids in base fluid on heat transfer applications. Fig. 9a, b, and c represent the experimental results of thermal conductivity of carbon-black in water-based nanofluid in this study and reference value of water [55,68]. The study confirmed the enhanced thermal conductivity of carbon-black nanofluid formulations (Line 1, Line 2, Coarse) compared to water, with calibration showing deviations below 1 % across three tests, as illustrated in Fig. 9a, b, and c. Thermal conductivity of the nanofluids increased with temperature, a phenomenon linked to increased kinetic energy from greater nanoparticle collisions—known as Brownian motion [68,69]. Brownian motion [50,71,72,73] is characterized by the erratic movements of particles suspended in a fluid, primarily due to ongoing collisions between nanoparticles and fluid molecules. This activity enhances the heat transfer mechanisms, significantly improving the thermal conductivity of the nanofluid system [71,74]. There is a positive correlation between the nanoparticle concentration in the nanofluid and its thermal conductivity—the more nanoparticles, the higher the conductivity. Increasing the nanoparticle concentration boosts collision frequency, enhancing kinetic activity and heat transfer efficiency. Analysis of three types of carbon-black nanofluids—Line 1, Line 2, and Coarse—revealed distinct trends linked to nanoparticle sizes of 30, 80, and 200 nm, respectively. These trends illustrate that smaller nanoparticles, due to their higher interfacial area, enhance the thermal conductivity more effectively [75]. Thus, optimizing nanoparticle size and concentration in nanofluids can substantially improve their thermal performance, underscoring the critical role of these factors in nanofluid design and application.

The study assessed the thermal conductivity enhancement of carbon-black water-based nanofluids, revealing significant improvements. Line 1 demonstrated the highest enhancement, with increases ranging from 2.05 % to 8.12 % across temperatures from 35°C to 55°C. Comparatively, Line 2 and Coarse showed lower enhancements. Overall, carbon-black nanofluids enhanced thermal conductivity by 0.83 % to 8.12 % over pure water. These findings indicate that carbon-black water-based nanofluids exhibited a higher thermal conductivity enhancement than Al2O3 water-based nanofluids, as reported in previous studies by Sahamifar et al. [22] and Zacarías et al. [26]. Specifically, the thermal conductivity enhancement of carbon-black water-based nanofluids surpassed the range of 2 % to 5 % observed in Al2O3 water-based nanofluids within the weight concentration range of 0.02 % to 10 %, which encompasses the parameters considered in the current study. Furthermore, CNT water-based nanofluids showed an even greater enhancement in thermal conductivity, with increases ranging from 6 % to 25 % compared to pure water, as reported by Estellé et al. [24] and Ueki et al. [25]. And the others SiO2, TiO2, ZrO2, CuO, SiC and ZrO2 water-based nanofluids as reported by Pryazhnikov et al. [23] and Ajeena et al [27]. provided 2 % to 20 % enhancement compared to pure water. The results of this study indicate that the carbon-black water-based nanofluids also exhibited competitive thermal conductivity enhancement, further supporting the potential of these nanofluids for thermal management applications.

The specific heat described as the amount of heat that is absorbed by a unit mass of a material, resulting in a temperature increase by 1 unit [60]. This study additionally conducted measurements of specific heat to complement the thermal properties analysis, validating the results against a reference point, as illustrated in Fig. 10 [70]. The results of the measurements indicated a significant degree of precision, as the deviations were consistently less than 1 %. An increase in specific heat is evident with a little increase in temperature. Theoretically, specific heat is expected to be temperature-dependent due to Brownian motion, similar to thermal conductivity [71,72,73,75,76,77]. The increased kinetic energy of particles at elevated temperatures leads to a higher thermal absorption capacity, thereby enhancing the efficiency of energy storage and release as temperature rises. The specific heat of carbon-black was found to be lower than that of water. Additionally, it was observed that as the weight concentration of carbon-black nanoparticles increased, there was a corresponding decrease in the specific heat. When evaluating the specific heat capacities of pure carbon-black nanoparticles and water as a medium, as referenced in [78,79], it was determined that the values were approximately 1000 - 2000 J/kgK and 4182 J/kgK, respectively. The specific heat capacity of carbon-black nanofluids was determined to lie between the values of carbon-black nanoparticles and water. An increase in the concentration of nanoparticles within the medium resulted in a trend towards the specific heat capacity of carbon-black nanoparticles in the nanofluid. One potential explanation for this phenomenon could be attributed to the aggregation of colloidal solids, specifically carbon-black, within nanofluids due to intermolecular forces. This aggregation leads to a reduction in the surface area of the colloidal carbon-black, thereby impacting its heat capacity [80]. Furthermore, the size of nanoparticle also affects to the specific heat by the effect of surface area. The size of nanoparticles plays a crucial role in determining the surface-to-volume ratio, with smaller particles having a larger solid(carbon-black)/liquid(water) interface area for the same volume concentrations as shown in Fig. 10a, b, and c. As a result the specific heat increased, suspensions containing smaller nanoparticles exhibit enhanced interfacial effects due to the greater surface area available for interactions between the solid phase, carbon-black, and liquid phase, water [81].

The results depicted in Fig. 10 indicate a notable reduction in the specific heat of carbon-black water-based nanofluids. Line 1 displayed a decrease of approximately 3.7 % at a weight concentration of 0.005 %, 4.61 % at 0.01 % weight concentration, 5.51 % at 0.02 % weight concentration, and 6.46 % at 0.03 % weight concentration. Similarly, Line 2 exhibited a decrease of around 4.28 % at 0.005 % weight concentration, 4.93 % at 0.01 % weight concentration, 5.08 % at 0.02 % weight concentration, and 5.85 % at 0.03 % weight concentration. Coarse particles experienced a decrease of 1.65 % at 0.005 % weight concentration, 2.52 % at 0.01 % weight concentration, 3.99 % at 0.02 % weight concentration, and 5.12 % at 0.03 % weight concentration. These findings underscore the influence of different weight concentrations on the specific heat of nanofluids, suggesting the need for further research in this area. In comparison to a study by Ueki et al. [25] which reported a specific heat decrease of 2 % to 10 % in CNT water-based nanofluids compared to pure water at 0.5 % to 1.5 % volume concentration specific heat, and another study by Sunder et al. [28] which measured Fe3O4 water-based nanofluids at 0 % to 2 % volume concentration showing a specific heat decrease of 0.2 % to 2 % compared to pure water, the carbon-black water-based nanofluids examined in this study exhibited competitive results. Further investigation into the specific heat properties of these nanofluids could provide valuable insights for potential applications in various fields.

The hydrodynamic properties under consideration in this study include viscosity and density of carbon-black nanoparticle in water-based, as depicted in Figs. 11 and 12
, respectively. In Figs. 11 and 12, the tag a, b, and c referred to Line 1, Line 2, and Coarse, respectively. The findings of the water analyzed in the study were graphically represented by black square symbols (▪), with reference values indicated by cross symbols (×). The result of the carbon-black nanofluids were denoted as followed 0.005 % weight in triangle symbol (▴), 0.01 % weight in circle symbol (ο), 0.02 % weight in diamond symbol (◊), 0.03 % weight in white square symbol (□). The calibration process of viscosity and density measurement resulted in an average deviation of approximately 0.5 %. Furthermore, the margin of deviation in the measurements consistently remained below 1 % throughout the three repetitions of the experiment. Viscosity is a crucial characteristic that significantly influences fluid dynamics, particularly in terms of the pressure drop experienced during motion in any direction. And viscosity can be defined as the ratio of shearing stress to the velocity gradient within a given unit area [61]. The dispersion of carbon-black in water-based has been noted to have a considerable influence on viscosity, as demonstrated in Fig. 11a – c. Specifically, the viscosity of the carbon-black nanofluid was observed to be elevated compared to that of pure water, with the maximum recorded value observed at a concentration of 0.03 % weight of Coarse type nanoparticles at 35°C, exhibiting an increase of approximately 11 %. Conversely, a decrease in viscosity was observed with an increase in temperature. The trend of increasing viscosity with higher concentrations and decreasing viscosity with higher temperatures was observed, which was also noted in the density measurements. Fig. 12a – c display the measurement results of water and the carbon-black in water-based nanofluid density. Density is operationally defined as the measure of an object's mass per unit volume [62]. From Fig. 12a – c, it was observed that the carbon-black in water-based nanofluid had the effects from the concentration, temperature, and particle size. The elevated concentration of carbon-black nanoparticles in water led to a corresponding rise in the density of the nanofluid, with a tendency for density to further increase at higher concentrations. Conversely, elevated temperatures were found to result in a decrease in the density of the nanofluid, with a trend for density to further decrease at higher temperatures.

The findings regarding density indicate that there was likely no significant distinction among the three different sizes. An investigation into the influence of concentration, temperature, and particle size on the viscosity and density of a substance exhibited consistent results. The crucial factor to consider is the interaction between nanoparticles to nanoparticles and nanoparticles to water, as it significantly influences the characteristics and properties of the resulting fluids. The well dispersion of carbon-black nanoparticles in water serves to enhance intermolecular forces, ultimately resulting in an elevation in viscosity. There were three differences trends that effect from increased particle size as reported in previous studies [82] all including increased, remained and decreased the viscosity. This study results matched with the decreasing trend when increased the carbon-black particle size as shown in Fig. 11. One possible explanation for this observed trend may be attributed to a gap existing between carbon-black particles. As discussed in Section 2.3, the carbon bonds present in the particles necessitate the breaking of these bonds using acetone to disperse the particles in water. Consequently, carbon-black nanoparticles tend to form strong bonds with others. During the measurement of nanofluid viscosity using a cylindrical viscometer, a cylindrical spindle continuously stirs and mixes the nanofluid matrix. This mixing action brings nanoparticles into proximity with each other, thereby increasing the likelihood of bonding between them and subsequently leading to an increase in viscosity. Similarly, a rise in the concentration of nanoparticles resulted in heightened viscosity as a result of enhanced interactions between nanoparticles themselves as well as between nanoparticles and molecules of the base liquid [83]. On the contrary, increasing the temperature results in a reduction of viscosity and density due to the weakening of intermolecular forces between particles as well as between particles and water.

The viscosity of carbon-black water-based nanofluids was evaluated as shown in Fig. 11, with Line 1 exhibiting the most significant increase in viscosity across temperatures, ranging from 0.35 % to 24.21 %. Line 2 and Coarse also showed considerable increases, with Line 2 ranging from 2.45 % to 20.63 % and Coarse ranging from 1.39 % to 22.95 %. This comparison highlights Line 1′s pronounced viscosity enhancements at higher temperatures.

In comparison to a previous study presented in Table 1, the carbon-black water-based nanofluids exhibited favorable results by demonstrating lower viscosity within a concentration range of 0.005 % to 0.03 %, with an increase ranging from 0.35 % to 24.21 %. In contrast, Sunder et al. [28] observed a superior increase in viscosity ranging from 6 % to 108 % for Fe3O4 water-based nanofluids at a concentration of 0 % to 2 % volume, while Halelfadl et al. [36] reported a viscosity increase of CNT water-based nanofluids compared to pure water of up to 6 times at concentrations ranging from 0.0055 % to 0.55 % volume, which falls within the concentration range considered in this study.

The findings presented in Fig. 12 illustrate a notable trend in the density of carbon-black water-based nanofluids. It was observed that the maximum increase in density occurred within a weight concentration range of 0.005 % to 0.03 %, with a marginal increase of approximately 1 %. This observation is consistent with previous studies on CNT water-based nanofluids conducted by Montazer et al. [40] and Halelfadl et al. [41] as documented in Table 1.

The analysis of thermophysical properties in Sections 4.1 and 4.2 highlighted the significant impact of weight concentration, temperature, and particle size on thermophysical properties. But the trending and consequence effects seemingly hard to interpret all of them at the same time even some of them had linearity trend. The Analysis of Variance (ANOVA) was considered as the optimization method. The RSM design was utilized for considering the influence of concentration, temperature, and particle size on thermal conductivity, specific heat, viscosity, and density, individually. The correlation models were developed to represent the relationships between control factors, such as weight concentration, temperature, and particle size, and the thermophysical properties of the system as responses. The weight concentration and, temperature were numerical factors and particle size which also denoted to the type of Line 1, Line 2, and Coarse was the categorical factor. The correlation models were expressed through equations (A1) to (D3) in the Supplementary material. These equations were further categorized based on the types of Line 1, Line 2, and Coarse, with three equations representing each property. Findings from Sections 4.1 and 4.2 indicated that maximizing thermal properties such as thermal conductivity and specific heat lead to improve heat exchange efficiency. Conversely, minimizing hydrodynamic properties such as viscosity and density can help reduce pressure drop, which in turn lowers the required pumping power. The optimum points were resolved by multi-objective optimization that the criteria were represented in Table 4 . The primary objective of the optimization is to enhance the hydrothermal performance through the careful management of heat transfer and hydrodynamics with initial expense minimization on amount of nanoparticle and proper operating temperatures. The optimized results were presented in Table 5 , which detailed three distinct cases: Line 1, Line 2, and Coarse. The recommended concentration for achieving optimal results was found to be 0.01 % weight concentration, with a corresponding temperature of approximately 45°C. The impact of concentration and temperature on thermophysical properties was extensively examined and validated through the analysis of variance (ANOVA) results, which have been compiled and presented in Tables S3 to S6 in the Supplementary material. The crucial parameters that used for indicating the influence of control factors were F-value and P-value. The F-value in statistical analysis signifies the ratio of the variance among group means to the mean of the variances within groups, thereby indicating the proportion of explained variance relative to unexplained variance [84,85].

On the other hand, the P-value reflects the probability of obtaining test results consistent with a given statistical hypothesis test. The parameters to be considered would be chosen from the P-value below 0.005 or the highest F-values. The results of the study were analyzed using F and P-values. The data revealed that the control factor with the highest P-value had a significant impact on the viscosity and density of the carbon-black nanofluid was the temperature. This observation aligns with Figs. 11 and 12, which illustrates the fluctuations in viscosity and density of water and carbon-black nanofluid with changes in temperature, while maintaining relatively consistent results across different concentrations. Conversely, the specific heat of the carbon-black in water-based nanofluid was predominantly influenced by variations in concentration, as evidenced by the consistent findings in Fig. 10a – c. The significance of the thermal conductivity results was challenging to determine due to the proximity of the F-values associated with temperature and concentration, which were recorded as 1585.1 and 1689.63 which were presented on TableS3 in Supplementary material, respectively. The data suggested that concentration was expected to have a greater influence on thermal conductivity. This emphasizes the critical role of statistical analysis in elucidating the most influential factors in experimental outcomes.

The correlation models derived from analysis of variance (ANOVA) were utilized not only for assessing the influence of control factors, but also as predictive models for fluid thermophysical properties. CFD serves as a potent tool for evaluating performance and examining the transport phenomena of nanofluids. The incorporation of correlation models for thermal conductivity, viscosity, specific heat, and density in the CFD model will enable the accurate representation of the real-world behavior of both water and nanofluid as they respond to temperature variations. The current study utilized a temperature dependent CFD model for the purpose of validation and subsequent analysis. The microchannel plate heat exchanger geometry as detailed by Zhou et al. [66] was employed, with specific dimensions provided in Fig. 8 and Table S2 of the Supplementary material. This geometry was made to elucidate the benefits of utilizing carbon-black nanofluid within the heat exchanger system. To verify the consistency of CFD calculation results and to minimize computational resources, grid independence was employed. The verification was evaluated using the Nusselt number and pressure drop under the conditions of Re 300, with 30°C water as coolant and 80°C water as the hot fluid. The results in Table 6 show that the CFD calculations for Nusselt number and pressure drop begin to stabilize at 4 million grids, with both deviations less than 0.10 %. This indicates that the minimum number of grids required to achieve consistent results is 4 million. The validation results of previous study and this study were tabulated on Table 7 . The simulation result of this study preferred proximate simulated calculation result with real experimental result and previous simulation study which implanted the correlation model of water thermophysical properties which validated with Donald and Hobart [70,71]. The calculation results from CFD model agreed with the experimental results well and occurred the highest deviation at Re = 200 with 9.09 % on Nu and 7.69 % on ΔP. The validation results also depicted a variance in the Reynolds number, which may serve to preliminarily justify the appropriate operating Reynolds number. An analysis of the Nusselt number and pressure drop (ΔP) at Reynolds numbers ranging from 100 to 300, as indicated in Table 7, indicates notable increases in both parameters at Re = 200 compared to Re = 100. Specifically, the Nusselt number and pressure drop at Re = 200 increased by approximately two and five times, respectively. Moreover, at Re = 300, the Nusselt number and pressure drop (ΔP) exhibited further increments of 20 % (∼1.2 times) and 80 % (∼1.8 times), respectively, in comparison to Re = 200. The results indicate clear trends in heat transfer and hydrodynamics within the Reynolds number range of 100 to 200, demonstrating substantial increases in pressure drop and Nusselt number values across this interval. This suggests that optimal hydrothermal performance occurs within the Reynolds number range of 100 to 200 for plate microchannel heat exchangers of this size. This range of Reynolds numbers is also supported by Jamshidmofid et al. [86], who conducted a study on plate microchannel heat exchangers of similar dimensions and found similar results. For evaluating the hydrothermal efficiency of different nanofluids as presented in Table 5, this study selected to analyze the median data point at Reynolds number (Re) of 150.

The simulation cases were selected from optimized cases in Table 5. The boundary conditions were remained from the validation section with 30 °C in cold side fluid, 80 °C in hot side fluid, copper as solid body and used Re =150.

Fig. 13 represented an example from thermal conductivity advantage of nanofluid. The thermal conductivity would be raised when increased temperature as finding in Section 4.1. Fig. 13 contour shows the higher thermal conductivity of carbon-black nanofluid than water especially at the edge of the microchannel. At the edge of the microchannel is a conjugated interface that temperature gradients driving though from solid (the structure of microchannel heat exchanger as copper-based) to fluid (the hot water as heat source). So, the main heat transfer mode at this interface is heat conduction which heat conduction refers to the process by which heat is transferred through a medium due to a temperature gradient. This transfer of heat is directly proportional to the temperature difference between the two contacting mediums [88]. In the contact site between two mediums like at the edge of microchannel, the heat conduction was controlled by the interface thermal conductivity of those two mediums [89]. The better thermal conductivity fluid will prefer the better heat transfer rate and the contour in Fig. 13 also supported that. Overall, the thermal conductivity contour of carbon-black nanofluid presented the higher thermal conductivity value along the microchannel compared to water contour below. The thermal conductivity also represented the temperature at that point, due to thermal conductivity value will be varied to temperature. Consequently, the higher thermal conductivity tends to introduce a higher temperature profile as well. In addition, the high-profile temperature didn't only achieve by indicated thermal conductivity of the medium but also need to consider on specific heat. As Eq. (13) displayed the first law of the thermodynamics, the specific heat is the ratio of exchanged heat divided by ratio of mass flow rate multiplied temperature differences. Specific heat is the amount of heat that is absorbed by a unit mass of a material, resulting in a temperature increase by 1 unit and this related to temperature gradient in fluid's temperature profile. By this mean, the low specific heat can offer the good transfer rate by offer the thin thermal boundary layer [90]. The thickness of thermal boundary layer is indicated by dimensionless Prandtl number (Pr) which is the formula as follows
Pr =
C P
μ
k
. Prandtl number described the ratio of momentum diffusion to the thermal transport in the fluid. When Prandtl number equal to 1, it proposes the same thickness hydrodynamic boundary layer and thermal boundary layer. Pr > 1 a hydrodynamic boundary layer will thicker than a thermal boundary layer and Pr < 1 a hydrodynamic boundary layer should be thinner than a thermal boundary layer.

The hydrodynamic boundary layer and thermal boundary layer have conversed gradient in fully developed flow. The velocity is grading from the center of channel to the wall (velocity = 0 in no slip condition), while the temperature grading in converse. The convective heat transfer would fully perform when a hydrodynamic boundary layer will thicker than a thermal boundary layer by way of the thin temperature gradient offers steep gradient and thick velocity gradient provides high heat transfer rate. The steep gradient will offer high driving force to transfer the heat and heat greatly transferred by velocity thick velocity gradient, combinedly. Fig. 14 a shown the temperature contour of considered 0.01 % carbon-black nanofluid was shown on above and water in below, Fig. 14b expanded the temperature profile at the considered fully developed flow region. It was founded that the carbon-black nanofluid temperature profile was thinner than water. As calculated Pr number of studied cases were presence in the last column in Table 5, there was founded that the water case had the lowest calculated Pr number. The higher Pr number could be assumed that cases would have the higher hydrodynamic boundary layer and lower thermal boundary layer as well. The hydrothermal phenomena were more explicit in the microchannel scale by scaling effect [91,88] and nanofluid would perform outstanding as this study results suggested that in CFD results.

To indicate the hydrothermal performance of considered cases, heat transfer rate (Q) and Pumping power (PP) were calculated in Table 8 . The results in Table 8 suggested that all carbon-black nanofluids that were preliminary optimized by RSM can perform better performance in both heat transfer and hydrodynamics compared to the water case. They achieved higher heat transfer rates and needed lower required pumping power than water at the same operating condition. Based on the results presented in Table 8, it is evident that the heat transfer rate was highest in Case 3, followed by Case 1 and Case 2, respectively. In terms of pumping power, Case 2 and Case 3 exhibited the highest values, with Case 1 having slightly lower pumping power requirements. The hydrothermal performance of all cases was relatively similar, with Case 1 achieving the best overall performance due to its lower pumping power requirements and moderate heat transfer capabilities. The trend of hydrothermal performance is also respected to the Pr number as presented in Table 8. The Line 1 case preferred the best performance by achieving 9.05 % enhancement, the Coarse case achieved 8.31 %, and the Line 2 case was the last with 7.80 % when compared to the water case. The results also found that the proper operating conditions can help to achieve good performance. Furthermore, the findings of this study underscored the significance of optimizing the properties of carbon-black nanofluids. It was observed that achieving the higher thermal conductivity enhancement did not necessarily result in a better rate of heat transfer. A comparison between Case 2 and Case 3 revealed that although Case 2 exhibited a higher thermal conductivity, it had a lower specific heat compared to Case 3, which demonstrated superior heat transfer performance. This indicates that factors beyond thermal conductivity, such as specific heat, play a crucial role in determining thermal performance. Specifically, Case 3 was found to have a higher specific heat compared to other carbon-black nanofluid cases, suggesting that it struck a balance between thermal conductivity and specific heat. In terms of heat conduction, thermal conductivity plays a main role in facilitating heat transfer at the solid-fluid interface. On the other hand, specific heat serves as a parameter that signifies the temperature driving forces within a given body. Therefore, while Case 1 highlighted the importance of efficiently collecting heat from a source, thereby maximizing heat transfer potential, Case 3 underscored the significance of effectively delivering heat from the source to the fluid. The results of this study suggested that maximizing heat transfer potential tends to be more slightly effective than delivering heat from the source to the fluid.

Moreover, the findings in Sections 4.1 and 4.2 on carbon-black water-based nanofluid characterization exposed that carbon-black nanoparticle dispensing in water provided thermal conductivity enhancement and viscosity increment. These advantages and disadvantages tend to vary by concentration, particle size, and temperature. When particle size and concentration of all carbon-black nanofluid was equal, the temperature was the only factor that can be varied. As a coolant, the water and carbon-black nanofluids will receive the heat that was transferred from the hot water, and then their temperature increases. The rate of heat transfer in different coolants depended on thermophysical properties. Thermal conductivity played the main role in the heat transfer at the fluid-solid interface since heat conduction mode was the most influence there. Consequently, it affects the temperature profile, and a better thermal conductivity fluid can achieve a higher heat transfer rate. As shown in Fig. 14, the temperature profile of carbon-black nanofluid presented a better steep temperature gradient than water. Then, the temperature gradient will affect the other properties including viscosity, specific heat, and density. These emphasized the importance of the enhancement in thermal conductivity of nanofluid that controlled the consequence properties and phenomena [89,90,91]. In addition, utilizing a proper nanofluid with operating suitable conditions can achieve a better heat transfer rate and can reduce required pumping power even carbon-black nanoparticle dispensing in water increases the viscosity of nanofluid.

In this study, the carbon-black nanofluid was studied to characterize the thermophysical properties with the change of particle size of carbon-black, temperature, and concentration of carbon-black nanofluid. The response surface method (RSM) was employed to preliminary optimize the thermophysical properties of the characterized nanofluid to achieve the optimum point of hydrothermal performance by finding a minimum % weight concentration and the lowest operating temperature to minimize the required cost. The predictive correlation models were applied to the Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) model to develop a temperature-dependent CFD model for investigating newly characterized carbon-black nanofluids performance and their insight into hydrothermal phenomena.
-
The study findings indicate that temperature significantly affects the viscosity and density of carbon-black nanofluids, with these properties remaining relatively stable across different concentrations. However, specific heat is mainly influenced by concentration variations, while the impact on thermal conductivity is less clear due to closely related P-values for temperature and concentration.
-
Optimization data suggests that carbon-black nanofluids perform best at concentrations ranging from 0.005 % to 0.03 %. Using a 0.01 % concentration in microchannel heat exchangers resulted in superior heat transfer rates and pumping power ratios compared to water, providing valuable insights for performance optimization.
-
The Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) model was validated against empirical data, revealing a strong correlation with an average deviation of 4.73 % in pressure drop and 5.36 % in the Nusselt number, confirming the model's accuracy and reliability.
-
Simulation results demonstrated that carbon-black nanofluids could enhance heat transfer, primarily due to improved thermal properties and increased pressure drop from enhanced hydrodynamic properties, leading to higher heat transfer rates, reduced fluid viscosity, and lower pressure drops.
-
Further, using a 0.01 % concentration of carbon-black nanofluid at 45°C showed a remarkable improvement in heat transfer rates and pumping power ratios across different fluid lines compared to pure water, with increases of 9.05 %, 7.80 %, and 8.31 % respectively.

This study introduces a novel integration of experimental and simulation techniques to effectively investigate nanofluids. This approach not only serves as a practical tool but also offers crucial insights for performance planning and prediction prior to industrial application. Utilizing nanofluids in microchannel heat exchangers shows great potential for enhancing the utility of by-products while ensuring optimal hydrothermal performance. The use of carbon-black nanofluids appears to be an efficient, eco-friendly, and cost-effective option. Additionally, these findings align with the United Nations' Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), contributing to technologies that advance net-zero emissions and improve heat transfer processes, promote reuse, and reduce unwanted emissions. However, further research is necessary to examine the effects of factors such as roughness, particle shape, and aspect ratio on nanoparticle behavior and performance enhancement. Ongoing studies also aim to expand the use of these nanofluids in polymer-based microchannel heat exchangers to evaluate performance and develop sustainable systems.
